ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for the exact composite dish 'mango sticky rice with purple sweet potato'. Closest defensible anchor is the glutinous rice food product class. Purple sweet potato and mango components are not separately coded here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of mango sticky rice with purple sweet potato is approximately 300 g, comprising a portion of coconut-milk-soaked glutinous rice, sliced fresh mango, and boiled purple sweet potato chunks.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice (cooked), Mango (raw), Coconut Milk, Purple Sweet Potato (cooked); composite weighted estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), canned coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and cooked purple sweet potato (FDC ID 168482), consistent with the approximate proportions implied by the input record macros. Input per_100g values (energy 183 kcal, protein 2.0 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 28.33 g, sugar 15.0 g, sodium 50 mg, dietary fibre 1.33 g) are plausible for this composition and are retained with medium confidence. Calcium 6.67 mg, iron 0.33 mg, cholesterol 0.0 mg, saturated fat 5.33 g, trans fat 0.0 g, and vitamin C 10.0 mg are also retained from the input as consistent with USDA component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; glutinous rice (sticky rice) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for the exact composite dish 'mango sticky rice with purple sweet potato'. GI of 86 is taken from the published GI of glutinous (sticky) rice (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The mango and purple sweet potato components have lower individual GIs but represent a smaller carbohydrate fraction; the composite GI is dominated by the glutinous rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (86 × 28.33 g/100g × 300 g serving / 100) = 86 × 85 g / 100 ≈ 73.1, rounded to 73.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar load (15.0 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium content itself is low at 50 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 50 mg/100g, the high sugar content (15.0 g/100g) with minimal fibre (1.33 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, which are independent risk factors for hypertension; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and avoid frequent consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 86) from glutinous rice combined with 28.33 g/100g carbohydrate, 15.0 g/100g sugar, and only 1.33 g/100g dietary fibre produces a very high glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 73)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI (86), high sugar (15.0 g/100g), and very high glycemic load per standard serving (GL ≈ 73); if consumed at all, portion must be strictly limited to 50–80 g and paired with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk at 5.33 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold and may raise LDL cholesterol; high sugar load also contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 5.33 g/100g (predominantly from coconut milk) and high sugar load (15.0 g/100g) are both cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and consider requesting reduced-coconut-milk preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.33 g/100g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g pro-inflammatory threshold; high sugar (15.0 g/100g) further promotes inflammatory mediator production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 5.33 g/100g and sugar at 15.0 g/100g both promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Sticky Rice with Purple Sweet Potato (Thai: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วงกับมันเทศสีม่วง; Chinese: 芒果糯米饭配紫薯) is a Thai-origin dessert dish sold in Singapore under the menu name 'D2 Mango Sweet Heart'. It consists of glutinous (sticky) rice steamed and soaked in sweetened coconut milk, served alongside slices of ripe fresh mango and boiled purple sweet potato chunks. The glutinous rice is soft, chewy, and richly flavoured with coconut milk and sugar; the mango provides natural sweetness and acidity; and the purple sweet potato adds an earthy, mildly sweet flavour with a vibrant colour contrast. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 183 kcal, 2.0 g protein, 6.67 g fat (of which 5.33 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), 28.33 g carbohydrate, 15.0 g sugar, 1.33 g dietary fibre, and 50 mg sodium. The glycemic index is high (GI ≈ 86, based on glutinous rice reference data), making the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving approximately 73 — very high. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI and glycemic load, and is a concern for those with hypertension (high sugar driving insulin-mediated sodium retention), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from coconut milk), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sugar). The dish is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a popular dessert in Thai cuisine and is widely available at Thai dessert stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for glutinous rice, mango, coconut milk, and purple sweet potato, and are consistent with the input record values. GI is estimated from the published GI of glutinous rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than high because no direct approved-source match exists for the composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; coconut milk is predominantly saturated fat with minimal MUFA/PUFA. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in a single approved-source entry; USDA FoodData Central component data were used for macros only. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based (glutinous rice, coconut milk, mango, purple sweet potato). The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not an approved primary source; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on published glutinous rice GI (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (86 × 85.0 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 73.1, rounded to 73.0.
